reason: Both answers correctly identify CFAA as weak once login is avoided, breach of contract/ToS as the main exposure, hiQ's ultimate loss on contract grounds, state privacy/data-broker laws as secondary exposure, and the cease-and-desist-then-litigation pattern; neither claims public data is risk-free. Answer A is more actionable, offering concrete rules (never authenticate, don't evade blocks post-C&D, diversify data sources) and correctly adds the relevant Bright Data v. Meta (2024) ruling supporting the logged-out-scraping distinction, which B omits. A's Proxycurl narrative is more detailed, reinforcing the lesson with real consequences, at a small cost of added specificity. B is cleaner and more concise but frames guidance as questions rather than concrete rules and lacks the Bright Data counterpoint. Given comparable correctness, A edges out on decision-relevant specificity and actionability.
